Data Visualization

February 3, 2019

  • ggplot
    • Basics of ggplot
    • Aesthetic
    • Facets
    • Geometric objects
    • Statistical transformations (bar chart)
    • Other useful plots
    • Correlation plot
  • R Basic plot
    • Histogram
    • Bar Chart

    • Scatter Plot
    • R Graphic Options

Data visualization is the most straightforward way to get information from data. We will focus on ggplot2 to generate graphs because it is can produce the most elegant plot due to that ggplot2 implements the grammar of graphics, a coherent system for describing and building graphs. We will also talk about traditional R plot, which is way harder to make them look pretty, but sometime they are very easy to generate a plot.

ggplot

Load the ggplot2 package or tidyverse.

library(tidyverse)
## -- Attaching packages --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- tidyverse 1.2.1 --
## v ggplot2 3.0.0     v purrr   0.2.5
## v tibble  1.4.2     v dplyr   0.7.6
## v tidyr   0.8.1     v stringr 1.3.1
## v readr   1.1.1     v forcats 0.3.0
## -- Conflicts ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ tidyverse_conflicts() --
## x dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
## x dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()

We use an R built-in data mpg.

mpg
## # A tibble: 234 x 11
##    manufacturer model      displ  year   cyl trans      drv     cty   hwy fl    class  
##    <chr>        <chr>      <dbl> <int> <int> <chr>      <chr> <int> <int> <chr> <chr>  
##  1 audi         a4           1.8  1999     4 auto(l5)   f        18    29 p     compact
##  2 audi         a4           1.8  1999     4 manual(m5) f        21    29 p     compact
##  3 audi         a4           2    2008     4 manual(m6) f        20    31 p     compact
##  4 audi         a4           2    2008     4 auto(av)   f        21    30 p     compact
##  5 audi         a4           2.8  1999     6 auto(l5)   f        16    26 p     compact
##  6 audi         a4           2.8  1999     6 manual(m5) f        18    26 p     compact
##  7 audi         a4           3.1  2008     6 auto(av)   f        18    27 p     compact
##  8 audi         a4 quattro   1.8  1999     4 manual(m5) 4        18    26 p     compact
##  9 audi         a4 quattro   1.8  1999     4 auto(l5)   4        16    25 p     compact
## 10 audi         a4 quattro   2    2008     4 manual(m6) 4        20    28 p     compact
## # ... with 224 more rows

Basics of ggplot

Suppose we want to see how car’s MPG relates to engine size.

ggplot(data=mpg) +
  geom_point(mapping = aes(x= displ, y= hwy))

The first function ggplot(data=mpg) creates an empty plot, which is the first layer of you graph, just like you grab a white plain paper. Then, add another layer uning the function geom_point(). It stands for “geometric object - point”. Of course there are different types of geometric object, such as bars, curves, straight lines, boxes, etc. You will see!

Each geom function in ggplot2 takes a mapping argument. This defines how variables in your dataset are mapped to visual properties. aes stands for aesthetic, which specifies what data you want to map (draw). Therefore, the mapping argument is always paired with aes().

Here is a template to produce graphs using ggplot2

ggplot(data = <DATA>) + 
  <GEOM_FUNCTION>(mapping = aes(<MAPPINGS>))

Exercise

  1. Run ggplot(data=mpg). What do you see?
  2. Read the help for mpg, what does the variable drv describe?
  3. Make a scatter plot of hwy vs. cyl.
  4. What happens if you make a scatter plot of class vs. drv? Can you tell anything form it?
  5. Try a traditional R plot by running plot(mpg$class, mpg$drv). Why can’t it go through?
  6. What type of plot would be the best to display class and drv?

Aesthetic

Back to our first scatter plot, what points are of your special interests? What do you think?

You probably think these points are representing a specific class of cars. Let’s draw the same scatter plot but use different color to indicate different classes.

ggplot(data=mpg) +
  geom_point(mapping = aes(x= displ, y= hwy, color= class))

You are essentially adding (mapping) a third dimension class to the existing plot using colors (aesthetic). An aesthetic is a visual property of the objects in your plot. Aesthetics include things like the size, the shape, or the color of your points. The x and y locations of a point are themselves aesthetics, visual properties that you can map to variables to display information about the data.

Exercise

  1. Try to map class to other aesthetics: size, shape, and alpha, which controls the transparency of the points. Do you see any missed points?
  2. Replace color=class with color= displ>5. What do you get?
  3. Replace color=class with color= displ. What do you get?
  4. What happens if you map the same variable class to multiple aesthetics?

If you want to set color or shape manually, you need to specify them outside aes(). For example,

ggplot(data=mpg) +
  geom_point(mapping = aes(x= displ, y= hwy), color= "blue")

Here you don’t have the third dimension class, because you simple changed color for all points. You can also change the shape of points by specifying shape= or pch=.

ggplot(data=mpg) +
  geom_point(mapping = aes(x= displ, y= hwy), shape= 4)

Here are all shapes availabel in R.

Exercise

Run following code. Try to understand what it is doing. Then, check the help document by typing ?geom_point or go to here.

ggplot(data=mpg) +
  geom_point(mapping = aes(x= displ, y= hwy), shape=22, fill="red", size=3, stroke= 2)

Facets

Another way to draw plots by group is to draw different group seperately using facet_wrap(). This is extremely useful when you want to visualize data by groups.

ggplot(data = mpg) + 
  geom_point(mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy)) + 
  facet_wrap(~ class, nrow = 2)

Here, ~ stands for by, nrow simply specifies the number of rows of your plots.

You may also use facet_grid(a ~ b) to add another two dimensions a and b. For example, if you want to see the relation between displ and hwy by drv and cyl, you may use the code below. This is a four dimensional plot.

ggplot(data = mpg) + 
  geom_point(mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy)) + 
  facet_grid(drv ~ cyl)

Exercise

  1. Replace facet_grid(drv ~ cyl) with facet_grid(. ~ cyl) and facet_grid(drv ~ .), respectively. What do you get?

  2. What are the advantages to using faceting instead of the colour aesthetic? What are the disadvantages? How might the balance change if you had a larger dataset?

Geometric objects

A geom is the geometrical object that a plot uses to represent data. People often describe plots by the type of geom that the plot uses. For example, bar charts use bar geoms, line charts use line geoms, boxplots use boxplot geoms, and so on. The only difference between the two plots below is they are using different geometric objects, but exactly the same data.

These two plots are generated using the code below.

# left
ggplot(data = mpg) + 
  geom_point(mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy))
# right
ggplot(data = mpg) + 
  geom_smooth(mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy))

Here geom_smooth() tells R to draw a smooth curve as the geometric object. You may add the original data points in the same figure by using both geom_point() and geom_smooth() at the same time. You can also remove the confidence band by specifying se=FALSE

ggplot(data = mpg) + 
  geom_point(mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy)) +
  geom_smooth(mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy), se=FALSE)

Now, recall how you change the shape and color for scatter plot. Similarly, you can also change them for the line plot (e.g. solid line, dashed line, dotted line, etc.).

ggplot(data = mpg) + 
  geom_smooth(mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy), linetype=2, color=2)

Note that we do not use shape as it does not make sense for lines. Instead, we use linetype, which indicates whether it is a solid line, dahsed line or other types.

If you want to draw different lines for different group in the same figure, you need to specify linetype and color inside the aes().

ggplot(data = mpg) + 
  geom_smooth(mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy, linetype=drv, color=drv)) +
  geom_point(mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy, color=drv))

You may also change the smooth curve to a straight line, i.e., the line fitted based on a simple linear regression. You nned to specify method="lm".

ggplot(data = mpg) + 
  geom_smooth(mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy), method = "lm") +
  geom_point(mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy))

Global mapping

It might be more comvenient to use global mapping, which means you map the data points you want to plot at the beginning, in the ggplot() function. Then you don’t need to specify mapping=aes() in every geom(). Try the code below.

ggplot(data = mpg, mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy)) + 
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth()

If you want to change color for aesthetic, you do need to use mapping again but no need to specify x and y again.

ggplot(data = mpg, mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy)) + 
  geom_point(mapping = aes(color = class)) +
  geom_smooth()

There are over 30 geoms in ggplot2. The best way to learn these is the ggplot2 cheatsheet.

Exercise

  1. Run the code below. Interpret what the figure is showing. Why would we specify x = class, y = hwy?
ggplot(data = mpg, mapping = aes(x = class, y = hwy)) + 
  geom_boxplot()

  1. How to show different boxplot using different color?

  2. Will these two graphs look different? Why/why not?

ggplot(data = mpg, mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy)) + 
  geom_point() + 
  geom_smooth()

ggplot() + 
  geom_point(data = mpg, mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy)) + 
  geom_smooth(data = mpg, mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy))
  1. Recreate the R code necessary to generate the following graphs.

Statistical transformations (bar chart)

Bar chart is a very useful chart to describe data frequency which is of common interest for descriptive analysis. We illustrate bar chart using diamonds dataset from ggplot2 package.

diamonds
## # A tibble: 53,940 x 10
##    carat cut       color clarity depth table price     x     y     z
##    <dbl> <ord>     <ord> <ord>   <dbl> <dbl> <int> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>
##  1 0.23  Ideal     E     SI2      61.5    55   326  3.95  3.98  2.43
##  2 0.21  Premium   E     SI1      59.8    61   326  3.89  3.84  2.31
##  3 0.23  Good      E     VS1      56.9    65   327  4.05  4.07  2.31
##  4 0.290 Premium   I     VS2      62.4    58   334  4.2   4.23  2.63
##  5 0.31  Good      J     SI2      63.3    58   335  4.34  4.35  2.75
##  6 0.24  Very Good J     VVS2     62.8    57   336  3.94  3.96  2.48
##  7 0.24  Very Good I     VVS1     62.3    57   336  3.95  3.98  2.47
##  8 0.26  Very Good H     SI1      61.9    55   337  4.07  4.11  2.53
##  9 0.22  Fair      E     VS2      65.1    61   337  3.87  3.78  2.49
## 10 0.23  Very Good H     VS1      59.4    61   338  4     4.05  2.39
## # ... with 53,930 more rows

Suppose we want to know the frequecy across different cut, we can draw the following bar chart.

ggplot(data = diamonds) + 
  geom_bar(mapping = aes(x = cut))

Here we don’t need y in the aes(), because geom_bar() automatically calculates the count in each category. In another words, geom_bar() first obtains the table below.

## 
##      Fair      Good Very Good   Premium     Ideal 
##      1610      4906     12082     13791     21551

And then, draws five bars with these five values. That is the step of statistical transformation.

However, if you don’t have the original data, but only the counts, to draw the same bar chart, you need to specify stat=identify, which stands for directly mapping the numbers as the height of bars. Try code below.

demo <- tribble(
  ~cut,         ~freq,
  "Fair",       1610,
  "Good",       4906,
  "Very Good",  12082,
  "Premium",    13791,
  "Ideal",      21551
)

ggplot(data = demo) +
  geom_bar(mapping = aes(x = cut, y = freq), stat = "identity")

geom_bar() associates with stat_count(), which calculates the frequency table that are used to draw the bar chart. Therefore, the two set of code below are equivalent.

ggplot(data = diamonds, mapping = aes(x = cut)) + 
  geom_bar()

ggplot(data = diamonds, mapping = aes(x = cut)) + 
  stat_count()

There are other equivalences between geom_ and stat_. For example, stat_summary(), by default produces vertical bar to indicate 1 standard error of the mean of y in each group x. Similar chart can be produced by geom_pointrange() (Exercise).

ggplot(data = diamonds, mapping = aes(x = cut, y=price)) + 
  stat_summary()
## No summary function supplied, defaulting to `mean_se()

Color and position of the bar chart

To add colors for each bar, you can use fill in the aes().

ggplot(data = diamonds) + 
  geom_bar(mapping = aes(x = cut, fill=cut))

We can map another dimension by using fill.

ggplot(data = diamonds) + 
  geom_bar(mapping = aes(x = cut, fill=clarity))

Now every single color represents different level of clarity.

You might think the following plot is better to present the same information.

ggplot(data = diamonds) + 
  geom_bar(mapping = aes(x = cut, fill = clarity), position = "fill")

position="fill" makes all bars to have the same height, so that we can better compare the proportion of each clarity across different cut.

You may also seperate different clarity to different bars instead of stacking them together.

ggplot(data = diamonds) + 
  geom_bar(mapping = aes(x = cut, fill = clarity), position = "dodge")

Exercise

  1. Run the code below, what is the difference between the one you get and our first bar chart?
ggplot(data = diamonds) + 
  geom_bar(mapping = aes(x = cut, y=..prop.., group=1))

  1. Why do we need group=1? What happens if we remove it?

  2. How to get the bar chart below?

  3. Use stat_summary() or geom_bar() to generate the plot below.

  1. Try the code below and figure out what is going on.
bar <- ggplot(data = diamonds) + 
  geom_bar(mapping = aes(x = cut, fill = cut)) + 
  labs(x = "Cut", y = "Count", title="Frequency for different cut") +
  theme(aspect.ratio = 1, 
        legend.position = "none", 
        plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5)
        )

bar + coord_flip()
bar + coord_polar()

Other useful plots

So far, we have seen scatter plot, box plot, and bar chart. There are many other useful plots for data visualization.

Line plot

x<- seq(-8,8, length.out = 200)
y<- 1/(1+exp(-x))
ggplot(data.frame(x,y))+
  geom_line(aes(x, y), color="red", size=2)+
  geom_line(aes(x, 1), lty="dashed")

Try the code below.

x<- seq(-pi/2, pi/2, 0.001)
y<- (sqrt(cos(x))*cos(200*x)+sqrt(abs(x))-0.7)*(4-x^2)^0.01

ggplot(data.frame(x,y), aes(x, y))+
  geom_line(color="red")

Histogram

ggplot(data = diamonds) + 
  geom_histogram(aes(price), bins = 20, fill="blue")

Correlation plot

We can use package corrplot to draw correlation plot, which is more like a heat map to indicate the level of correlation. It is neither from ggplot nor R basic graphic. However, you may use ggplot plot or R basic graphic to generate similar plot. Not that correlation plot requires all variables to be numeric.

install.packages("corrplot")
library(corrplot)
M<- cor(mtcars)
corrplot(M, method="circle")

R Basic plot

Histogram

Histogram is the easiest way to show how numerical variables are distributed.

Produce a single histogram

hist(iris$Sepal.Length, col="green", breaks=20)

You may change “breaks=” and “col=” to have different appearance.

Density plot – Fitted curve for histogram

Density plot is a nonparametric fitting.

plot(density(iris$Sepal.Length))

Combine the histogram and the density chart.

You can make the plot more elegant with different options. For example, adding a title, adjusting the axis range, renaming the axis label, and so on…

You can also add curves on top of an existing plot by using lines() or abline() function.

hist(iris$Sepal.Length, prob=T, col="green", breaks=20, main="Histogram and Density of Sepal Length", xlim=c(3,9), xlab="Sepal Length")
lines(density(iris$Sepal.Length), col="red", lwd=2)

# Add a vertical line that indicates the average of Sepal Length
abline(v=mean(iris$Sepal.Length), col="blue", lty=2, lwd=1.5)

Bar Chart

Bar chart is produces by using a vector of single data points, which is often a vector of summary statistics. Therefore, you need to preprocess your data, and get summary statistics before drawing the bar chart.

# bar chart for average of the 4 quantitative variables
aveg<- apply(iris[,1:4], 2, mean)
barplot(aveg, ylab = "Average")

Exercise

Use ?barplot or Google search to produce following bar chart.

Pie Chart

Pie chart is commonly used to visualize the proportion of different subject. It is similar to bar chart. You have to use a vector of single data points to produce a pie chart.

pie(table(iris$Species), col=rainbow(3))

Box plot

Box plot can only be drawn for continuous variable.

# box plot of Sepal.Length
boxplot(iris$Sepal.Length)

Draw box plot of multiple variables into one figure

boxplot(iris[,1:4], notch=T, col=c("red", "blue", "yellow", "grey"))

Box plot by group

boxplot(iris[,1]~iris[,5], notch=T, ylab="Sepal Length", col="blue")

Scatter Plot

Simple Scatter plot of two numerical variables

plot(iris$Sepal.Length, iris$Sepal.Width, xlab = "Length", ylab = "Width", main = "Sepal")

Scatter plot matrix (all paired variables)

pairs(iris[,1:4])

R Graphic Options

You may display multiple plots in one window (one figure).

# set arrangement of multiple plots
par(mfrow=c(2,2))
# set mrgins
par(mar=c(4.5, 4.2, 3, 1.5)) 
hist(iris$Sepal.Length, xlab = "Sepal Length", cex.lab=1.5)
hist(iris$Sepal.Width, xlab = "Sepal Width", col = "red")
plot(iris$Sepal.Length, iris$Sepal.Width, xlab = "Length", ylab = "Width", main= "Sepal", pch=17)
boxplot(iris[,1:4], notch=T, col=c("red", "blue", "yellow", "grey"))

There are much more options that can make your plot nice. You can learn options at here or ask your best friend – Google.

Details about figure margins can be found here.
